narrative:
On March 3, 2026, President Trump hosted German Chancellor Friedrich Merz at the White House for what multiple sources describe as Trump's first in-person meeting with a world leader since the start of the U.S. war against Iran [2][4][6]. The meeting took place in the Oval Office, with Merz staying overnight at the White House guest house [2][4][5].

## Iran Conflict Dominates Discussion

Trump provided extensive updates on the military campaign against Iran, claiming significant damage to Iranian capabilities. He stated that Iran's "navy has been knocked out," "they have no air force," "no air detection," and "their radar has been knocked out" [2][4][5][6]. Trump characterized the military operation as proceeding "very well" and praised U.S. military performance [2][4][5][6].

Both leaders expressed alignment on Iran policy, with Merz stating they are "on the same page in terms of getting this terrible regime in Tehran away" [2][4][5][6]. However, Merz emphasized the need to discuss "the day after" and "what will happen then if they are out," suggesting focus on post-conflict planning [2][4][5][6]. Merz also stressed the importance of a "common approach" that includes security for the U.S., Europe, and Israel [1].

When asked whether Israel forced the U.S. into the conflict, Trump responded: "No, I might have forced their hand," explaining that he believed Iran "was going to attack first" based on ongoing negotiations [4][5]. Trump stated he "didn't want that to happen" [5].

## European Support Mixed

Trump acknowledged that European support for the Iran operation has been uneven. He praised Germany's assistance, telling reporters that some European countries "such as Germany, have been helpful, but others, such as Spain have not" [1]. Trump specifically criticized the UK as "uncooperative," complaining about Britain's decision "to not let the US use its bases to attack Iran" [1]. The New York Times noted that "Europeans did not take part in" the attack [7].

## Ukraine Tensions

The Ukraine conflict emerged as a point of subtle disagreement. Merz emphasized that "Ukraine has to preserve its territory and their security interests" [1][2][4][5][6], while acknowledging that "we all want to see this war coming to an end as soon as possible" [1][2][4][5][6].

Trump described Ukraine as "very high" on his priority list but admitted surprise at the difficulty of finding a solution, saying "I thought it was going to be one of the easier ones" [1]. He made what DW characterized as "the false claim that he has already settled eight wars in his second term" [1]. Regarding ceasefire negotiations, Trump said "Sometimes I blame one, sometimes I blame the other one," but expressed confidence that "a deal was going to happen" [1].

DW noted that a previous White House ceasefire proposal "had offered up Ukrainian land to Russia, including land that was not under Russian control," which "had sparked a heated response from European partners" [1].

## Trade and Bilateral Relations

Despite no clear progress on U.S. tariffs—described by DW as "one of the major goals of the Merz trip"—Trump praised the German Chancellor extensively [1]. He called Merz a "friend" who is "doing a very good job" and "a very, very successful man" [1][2]. Trump stated that the U.S. and Germany "get along very well" and have "a great affinity for each other" [2][6].

Merz expressed desire for a trade agreement "to be in place as soon as possible" [2][4][5][6], and Trump mentioned they would be "talking about some very big trade deals" [2][4][5][6].

keyDevelopments: First face-to-face meeting between Trump and a world leader since Iran war began
Trump claims extensive damage to Iranian military capabilities including navy, air force, and radar systems
Mixed European support revealed, with Germany helpful but UK and Spain criticized as uncooperative
Subtle disagreement on Ukraine territorial integrity vs. ceasefire urgency
No apparent progress on German trade/tariff concerns despite being a major goal of the visit
Trump suggests U.S. may have initiated Iran conflict to prevent Iranian first strike
